title: The Happy Prince
author: After Oscar Wilde
paragraphs: Once upon a time, high above an old city, a statue of a young prince stood on a tall pillar. He was covered from head to toe in thin leaves of gold. His eyes were two bright sapphires, and a large ruby glowed on the hilt of his sword. Everyone who passed below called him the Happy Prince, for he looked so splendid and so content.

When the prince was alive, he had lived inside a palace with walls so high that he never once saw what lay beyond them. He spent his days dancing and laughing, and everyone told him he was the happiest person in the whole kingdom. But now that he had become a statue, standing far above the rooftops, he could see every corner of the city and all the sorrow hidden within it.

One autumn evening, a little swallow flew over the city. His friends had left for Egypt weeks ago, but he had stayed behind because he was fond of a slender reed by the river. Now the air was turning cold, and he knew it was time to go. He stopped to rest between the feet of the golden statue.

Just as the swallow was closing his eyes, a drop of water fell on his head. He looked up in surprise. The sky was perfectly clear, yet tears were rolling down the prince's golden cheeks. 'Why are you crying?' the swallow asked softly.

The prince replied, 'When I was alive, I knew nothing of sadness. But now I stand so high that I can see it all. Down in that narrow lane, a little boy lies sick with fever. His mother has nothing to give him, not even a cup of broth. Please, little swallow, will you take the ruby from my sword and bring it to her?'

The swallow hesitated. Egypt was waiting, and the warm winds, and his friends. But the prince looked so sorrowful that his heart softened. He plucked the ruby from the sword with his beak, flew over the rooftops, and slipped through the window of the tiny house. He laid the jewel beside the mother's thimble. The sick boy stirred in his sleep and smiled. When the swallow flew back, he felt a curious warmth inside him, even though the night was cold.

The next morning, the swallow was ready to leave. But the prince spoke again. 'Across the city, in a drafty attic, a young writer is trying to finish a play. He is so cold and hungry that his fingers can barely hold the pen. Will you take one of my sapphire eyes and bring it to him?' The swallow said, 'But then you will have only one eye.' Still, he could not refuse, and he carried the sapphire through the frosty air to the shivering young man.

Another day passed. This time the prince asked the swallow to take his other sapphire eye to a little match girl in the square. Her matches had fallen into the gutter, and she was afraid to go home empty-handed. The swallow wept. 'If I do this, you will be completely blind.' The prince answered gently, 'It does not matter. Please, be my eyes instead.'

And so the swallow stayed. Each day he flew across the city and told the prince everything he saw. Whenever he described someone in need, the prince asked him to peel off another leaf of gold and carry it to them. One leaf for a hungry old woman, another for children huddled under a bridge. Leaf by leaf, the gold disappeared, until the prince stood gray and dull against the winter sky.

The cold grew fierce. Snow fell thick upon the rooftops. The little swallow shivered, but he would not leave the prince alone. He felt his wings growing heavy and knew that his time was running short. With his last strength, he flew up to the prince's shoulder and whispered, 'Dear prince, will you let me kiss your hand?' The prince said happily, 'I am so glad you are going to Egypt at last.'

The swallow shook his head. 'I am not going to Egypt. I am going somewhere much farther away.' He pressed his beak to the prince's hand, then fluttered gently down to his feet and lay still. At that very moment, a sharp crack rang out from inside the statue. The prince's heart, which was made of lead, had broken in two.

The next morning, the mayor walked by and saw how shabby the statue looked, stripped of its gold and jewels. He ordered it torn down and thrown into a furnace. But the cracked lead heart would not melt, no matter how hot the fire burned. The workers tossed it onto a dust heap, where the body of the little swallow already lay.

That day, God said to his angels, 'Go to that city and bring me the two most precious things you can find.' The angels searched every street and every house, and in the end they returned with the broken lead heart and the small, still bird. God smiled and said, 'You have chosen well. In my garden of paradise, this little bird shall sing forever, and this prince shall praise all that is beautiful for eternity.'

And so, dear listener, the Happy Prince and his faithful swallow teach us that true beauty is not found in gold or gemstones, but in a heart that gives freely. The night is deep now, and the moon is shining softly just for you. Close your eyes, hold kindness in your heart, and let sleep carry you gently away. Goodnight, and sweet dreams.
